notes: This keeps the current best braided-return-tolls terrain but splits each home base into the upper and lower scoring tiles, leaving A5/I5 as non-scoring start tiles behind the home wall. The hypothesis is that carrier returns must make one extra visible route commitment after surviving the tolls, which may improve high-search timing and interception decisions while preserving the known length, horizon, and fairness strengths of the return-toll layout.
conclusion: This map set a new high score of 71.87, improving on braided-return-tolls-v1 by raising ladder separation from 0.687 to 0.719 while preserving full horizon, side-fairness, turn-cap, and length credits. Splitting each home base into A4/A6 and I4/I6 kept median length at 13 with zero turn caps and a perfect 0.500 blue mirror winrate, so the added scoring commitment did not create replay or fairness risk. The hypothesis was mostly right: forcing an upper/lower return commitment improved the weighted Elo gap to 254, especially by increasing low-to-mid separation to 358 Elo, though the mid-to-high gap fell from 326 to 269. The surprising downside is that mcts-low became even worse relative to greedy at -436 Elo, but because negative gaps clip out, the stronger low-to-mid gain still improved the score. A concrete next experiment would keep the split home bases and try to recover top-rung separation with a small central timing feature that does not add optional flank fire.
